edges.description: Colonization permits expression of bacterial factors that promote tissue invasion and immune modulation.
Virulence factors trigger early host epithelial danger responses.
Early epithelial immune activation precedes and supports neutrophil-dominant inflammatory influx.
Activated neutrophils drive suppurative follicular pathology.
Suppurative follicular inflammation produces papular lesions.
Purulent follicular lesions emerge with ongoing suppurative inflammation.
Inflammatory follicular lesions frequently produce itch.
Diverse folliculotropic pathogens can trigger convergent inflammatory pathways.
Antibiotic pressure can precede overgrowth of gram-negative folliculotropic organisms.
edges.source: Prolonged broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure in acne or rosacea

edges.description: Gram-negative follicular infection converges on suppurative follicular inflammation.
Colonized follicles show higher rates of histologic inflammation.
Commensal colonization can shift toward pathogenic behavior under permissive host-agent conditions.
edges.target: Pathogenic Malassezia transition with virulence factor production

edges.description: Virulence factor production promotes symptomatic follicular inflammatory disease.
edges.source: Pathogenic Malassezia transition with virulence factor production

edges.description: Malassezia-driven inflammation contributes to follicular pustule formation.
Malassezia-associated inflammatory lesions are characteristically pruritic.
Eosinophilic inflammatory lesions are strongly pruritic.
edges.source: Eosinophilic follicular inflammation in advanced HIV

edges.description: Dysbiotic follicular microbiota is associated with abnormal host immune response profiles.
edges.target: Impaired antibacterial cytokine response in folliculitis decalvans

edges.description: Aberrant immune response contributes to persistent neutrophilic inflammatory disease with scarring outcomes.
edges.source: Impaired antibacterial cytokine response in folliculitis decalvans
edges.target: Neutrophilic scarring follicular inflammation in folliculitis decalvans

edges.description: Progressive scarring follicular inflammation causes permanent hair loss.
edges.source: Neutrophilic scarring follicular inflammation in folliculitis decalvans

edges.description: EGFR pathway disruption triggers cell-intrinsic hypersensitivity to inflammatory signaling.
edges.source: EGFR pathway disruption in follicular stem-cell niche
edges.target: JAK-STAT1 pathway hypersensitivity in EGFR-deficient follicles

edges.description: Heightened JAK-STAT1 signaling synergizes with interferon-γ-driven lymphoid inflammation.
edges.source: JAK-STAT1 pathway hypersensitivity in EGFR-deficient follicles
edges.target: Interferon-γ CD8 T-cell and NK-cell inflammatory amplification

edges.description: Inflammatory amplification damages the follicular stem-cell niche and drives scarring destruction.
edges.source: Interferon-γ CD8 T-cell and NK-cell inflammatory amplification
edges.target: Stem-cell niche compromise and scarring follicle destruction

edges.description: Scarring follicular destruction leads to permanent hair loss phenotypes.
edges.source: Stem-cell niche compromise and scarring follicle destruction

nodes.description: Follicular inflammation progresses to acute suppuration with histologic evidence of neutrophil-rich tissue injury.

evidence.reference_title: Hot tub dermatitis: a familial outbreak of Pseudomonas folliculitis.
evidence.snippet: Skin biopsies showed an acute, suppurative folliculitis and dermal abscess formation.

nodes.description: Scarring alopecia can occur in chronic folliculitis decalvans/tufted hair folliculitis.

evidence.reference_title: Management of Folliculitis Decalvans: A Systematic Review.
evidence.snippet: Folliculitis decalvans (FD) and Tufted Hair Folliculitis (THF) present with recurrent episodes of follicular inflammation, pustules and crusting, predominantly of the scalp, leading to scarring alopecia.

evidence.reference_title: Folliculitis Decalvans Has a Heterogeneous Microbiological Signature and Impaired Immunological Response.
evidence.snippet: Folliculitis decalvans (FD) is a rare primary neutrophilic scarring alopecia whose etiology has not been completely elucidated yet.

nodes.description: Demodex mites are present in a subset of follicles and are associated with inflammatory follicular histopathology.

evidence.reference_title: Demodex-associated folliculitis.
evidence.snippet: Demodex mites were found in 42% of follicles with inflammation, but in just 10% of the follicles without inflammation.

nodes.description: Follicles containing Demodex mites frequently show inflammatory changes, supporting a mite-associated inflammatory folliculitis pathway.

evidence.reference_title: Demodex-associated folliculitis.
evidence.snippet: Eighty-three percent of follicles with Demodex showed inflammation.

nodes.description: EGFR pathway loss removes protective signaling in the follicular stem-cell niche and predisposes to inflammatory tissue injury.
nodes.id: EGFR pathway disruption in follicular stem-cell niche

evidence.reference_title: JAK-STAT1 as therapeutic target for EGFR deficiency-associated inflammation and scarring alopecia.
evidence.snippet: In this study, we demonstrated the protective role of hair follicle-specific epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) against scarring hair follicle destruction.

nodes.description: In advanced HIV infection, a culture-negative eosinophilic folliculitis pattern can occur with chronic pruritic disease.
nodes.id: Eosinophilic follicular inflammation in advanced HIV

evidence.reference_title: Human immunodeficiency virus-associated eosinophilic folliculitis. A unique dermatosis associated with advanced human immunodeficiency virus infection.
evidence.snippet: These observations demonstrate that HIV-associated eosinophilic folliculitis is a unique HIV-related cutaneous disorder that is characterized by a culture-negative, chronic, pruritic folliculitis and a characteristic histopathologic picture.

nodes.description: Papular follicular eruption on hair-bearing skin, especially trunk and extremities in some subtypes.

evidence.reference_title: Hot tub dermatitis: a familial outbreak of Pseudomonas folliculitis.
evidence.snippet: The eruption consists of pruritic papules, papulopustules, nodules, and urticarial lesions on the trunk and extremities.

nodes.description: Folliculitis decalvans exhibits a disease-associated shift in follicular bacterial community structure.

evidence.reference_title: Folliculitis Decalvans Has a Heterogeneous Microbiological Signature and Impaired Immunological Response.
evidence.snippet: CONCLUSION: FD hair follicles have a specific heterogenous follicular bacterial microbiota signature.

nodes.description: Gram-negative organisms including Klebsiella, Escherichia coli, Enterobacter, and Proteus can overgrow and infect follicles.

evidence.reference_title: Bacteriologic and immunologic aspects of gram-negative folliculitis: a study of 46 patients.
evidence.snippet: The gram-negative organisms most frequently cultivated from nares, facial skin, and pustules were Klebsiella spp., Escherichia coli, Enterobacter spp., and Proteus spp.

nodes.description: Patients with folliculitis decalvans show reduced cytokine responses to bacterial stimulation.
nodes.id: Impaired antibacterial cytokine response in folliculitis decalvans

evidence.reference_title: Folliculitis Decalvans Has a Heterogeneous Microbiological Signature and Impaired Immunological Response.
evidence.snippet: IL-10, TNF-α, and IL-6 levels were significantly decreased in patients after incubation with various strains of bacteria compared with controls.
FD is a non-curable disease for which inflammatory pathways involving several cytokines as TNF, IL1β, IL8, TGFβ, IL12 and 23, could also play a role.

evidence.reference_title: Folliculitis decalvans.
evidence.snippet: Staphylococcus aureus and a deficient host immune response seem to play an important role in the development of this disfiguring scalp disease.

nodes.description: Interferon-γ-expressing CD8 T cells and NK cells amplify follicular inflammatory injury in EGFR-deficient settings.
nodes.id: Interferon-γ CD8 T-cell and NK-cell inflammatory amplification

evidence.reference_title: JAK-STAT1 as therapeutic target for EGFR deficiency-associated inflammation and scarring alopecia.
evidence.snippet: Mechanistically, disruption of EGFR signaling generated a cell-intrinsic hypersensitivity within the JAK-STAT1 pathway, which, synergistically with interferon gamma expressing CD8 T-cell and NK-cell-mediated inflammation, compromised the stem cell niche.

nodes.description: EGFR-deficient follicular cells develop heightened JAK-STAT1 signaling sensitivity.
nodes.id: JAK-STAT1 pathway hypersensitivity in EGFR-deficient follicles

evidence.reference_title: JAK-STAT1 as therapeutic target for EGFR deficiency-associated inflammation and scarring alopecia.
evidence.snippet: Mechanistically, disruption of EGFR signaling generated a cell-intrinsic hypersensitivity within the JAK-STAT1 pathway, which, synergistically with interferon gamma expressing CD8 T-cell and NK-cell-mediated inflammation, compromised the stem cell niche.

nodes.description: Keratinocytes act as early epithelial sentinels and initiate innate immune responses after bacterial challenge.

evidence.snippet: The immune response to S. aureus SSTIs involves initial responders such as keratinocytes and neutrophils, which are supported by dendritic cells and T-lymphocytes later during infection.

nodes.description: Malassezia yeast exists as part of the normal follicular mycobiome before symptomatic disease develops.

evidence.reference_title: Position statement: Recommendations on the diagnosis and treatment of Malassezia folliculitis.
evidence.snippet: Malassezia is a lipophilic yeast that is a part of the human mycobiome.

nodes.description: Symptomatic Malassezia folliculitis manifests as inflammatory pruritic papulopustular follicular lesions.

evidence.reference_title: Position statement: Recommendations on the diagnosis and treatment of Malassezia folliculitis.
evidence.snippet: Malassezia folliculitis appears when the benign colonization of the hair follicles, by the Malassezia yeasts, becomes symptomatic with pruritic papules and pustules.

nodes.description: Neutrophils are recruited and activated, amplifying follicular inflammatory injury.

evidence.snippet: The immune response to S. aureus SSTIs involves initial responders such as keratinocytes and neutrophils, which are supported by dendritic cells and T-lymphocytes later during infection.

nodes.description: Folliculitis decalvans is characterized by chronic neutrophilic follicular inflammation with scarring progression.
nodes.id: Neutrophilic scarring follicular inflammation in folliculitis decalvans

evidence.reference_title: Folliculitis Decalvans Has a Heterogeneous Microbiological Signature and Impaired Immunological Response.
evidence.snippet: BACKGROUND: Folliculitis decalvans (FD) is a rare primary neutrophilic scarring alopecia whose etiology has not been completely elucidated yet.

evidence.reference_title: Folliculitis decalvans.
evidence.snippet: Histology displays a mainly neutrophilic inflammatory infiltrate in early lesions and additionally lymphocytes and plasma cells in advanced lesions.

nodes.description: Gram-negative bacterial, fungal, parasitic, and viral pathogens can also colonize follicles and converge on inflammatory papulopustular disease.

evidence.reference_title: First step in the differential diagnosis of folliculitis: cytology.
evidence.snippet: In addition, several Gram-negative bacterial, fungal, parasitic, and viral pathogens can cause follicular papules and pustules.

nodes.description: Host-agent interactions can drive Malassezia transition from commensal to pathogenic states with production of inflammatory virulence factors.
nodes.id: Pathogenic Malassezia transition with virulence factor production

evidence.reference_title: Malassezia virulence factors and their role in dermatological disorders.
evidence.snippet: However, in certain conditions and individuals, it may transform into a pathogenic yeast with multiple associated dermatological disorders and various clinical manifestations.

nodes.description: Extended broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure can create conditions that favor gram-negative follicular infection.
nodes.id: Prolonged broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure in acne or rosacea

evidence.reference_title: Bacteriologic and immunologic aspects of gram-negative folliculitis: a study of 46 patients.
evidence.snippet: Gram-negative folliculitis is an infection with gram-negative rods that most often occurs as a complication of prolonged broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy in patients suffering from acne and rosacea.

nodes.description: Itching is common and can be severe in eosinophilic and Malassezia-associated variants.

evidence.reference_title: Hot tub dermatitis: a familial outbreak of Pseudomonas folliculitis.
evidence.snippet: The eruption consists of pruritic papules, papulopustules, nodules, and urticarial lesions on the trunk and extremities.

nodes.description: Follicular pustules are common in infectious and yeast-associated forms.

evidence.reference_title: First step in the differential diagnosis of folliculitis: cytology.
evidence.snippet: In addition, several Gram-negative bacterial, fungal, parasitic, and viral pathogens can cause follicular papules and pustules.

evidence.reference_title: Folliculitis decalvans.
evidence.snippet: Clinically, the lesions present with follicular pustules, lack of ostia, diffuse and perifollicular erythema, follicular tufting, and, oftentimes, hemorrhagic crusts and erosions.

nodes.description: S. aureus expresses cytolytic, superantigenic, and immune-evasion factors that enable progression from colonization to tissue-level inflammation.

evidence.snippet: In order to cause skin infections, S. aureus employs a host of virulence factors including cytolytic proteins, superantigenic factors, cell wall-anchored proteins, and molecules used for immune evasion.

nodes.description: S. aureus colonization of hair follicles is a common proximal trigger for infectious folliculitis.

evidence.reference_title: First step in the differential diagnosis of folliculitis: cytology.
evidence.snippet: There are various infectious and non-infectious causes of folliculitis, and the most common causative agent is Staphylococcus aureus.

nodes.description: Compromised follicular stem-cell niche integrity and associated signaling changes culminate in scarring follicular injury.
nodes.id: Stem-cell niche compromise and scarring follicle destruction

evidence.reference_title: JAK-STAT1 as therapeutic target for EGFR deficiency-associated inflammation and scarring alopecia.
evidence.snippet: Skin biopsies from EGFR inhibitor-treated and cicatricial alopecia patients revealed an active JAK-STAT1 signaling signature along with upregulation of antigen presentation and downregulation of key components of the EGFR pathway.
